coredata.dc:title: Acetophenone substituted phthalocyanines and their graphene quantum dots conjugates as photosensitizers for photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy against Staphylococcus aureus
coredata.dc:creator: Openda, Yolande Ikala
Sen, Pinar
Managa, Muthumuni
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: This work reports on the synthesis and characterization of novel acetophenone substituted phthalocyanines along with the self-assembled nanoconjugates formed via π-π stacking interaction between the synthesized unmetalated (2), zinc (3) and indium (4) phthalocyanines and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form 2@GQDs, 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs. The complexes and conjugates exhibited high singlet oxygen ranging from 0.20 to 0.79 in DMSO for Pcs and nanoconjugates where in all cases, the indium complexes showed the highest singlet oxygen quantum yields. The photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy activity of both phthalocyanines and nanoconjugates were tested against Staphylococcus aureus. 4@GQDs was found to be highly effective causing a 9.68 log reduction of the bacteria at 10 μM (based on Pc) when compared to 3.77 log reduction of 3@GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acetophenone phthalocyanine
Graphene quantum dots
Singlet oxygen
Photodynamic antimicrobial therapy
Staphylococcus aureus
Antimicrobial Photodynamic Therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Linkage of Pcs complexes to GQDs, (Scheme 2)
Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS)
Emission, Fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) and Fluorescence lifetime (τF)
Triplet quantum yield and triplet lifetime

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Organization for Women in Science for the Developing World
Department of Science and Technology, Government of Kerala

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) Innovation and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University, the Organization for Women in Science for the Developing World (OWSD) and Swedish International Development Cooperation Agency (Sida).

This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) Innovation and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University , the Organization for Women in Science for the Developing World (OWSD) and Swedish International Development Cooperation Agency (Sida) .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electronic absorbance spectra of phthalocyanine complexes (2-4) in DMSO.
Normalized absorption spectra of 3 and 3@GQDs in DMSO.
Normalized absorption (a) and emission (b) spectra of GQDs in DMF.
FT-IR spectra of Pc complexes and 3@GQDs as an example.
(A) TEM and (B) DLS images of GQDs and Pcs@GQDs nano-assemblies showing the morphology and size increase upon conjugation.
Example of normalized absorption, excitation and emission spectra of 3 in DMSO, excitation at 613 nm.
Fluorescence decay curve of 4 (as an example).
A typical spectrum for the determination of singlet oxygen quantum yield of (3) in DMSO using DPBF.
Transcient decay curve (using complex 4 as an example) in DMSO.
Dark toxicity studies (A) and photoinactivation of S.aureus (B) of Pcs alone and the Pcs@GQDs conjugates (irradiation at 670 nm).
Survival graphs of S. aureus incubated with 10 μM of photosensitizer, (A) for Pcs alone and (B) for the conjugates (irradiation at 670 nm).
Synthetic path of the MPcs and metal-free Pc.
Adsorption or π-π stacking of phthalocyanines on to graphene quantum dots.
Photophysicochemical parameters of the GQDs, Pcs alone and conjugates in DMSO.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are DLS sizes in nm.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are when exciting where GQDs absorb.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Log reduction and cell survival (%) values of 10 μM samples in 5 % DMSO/PBS after 120 min of irradiation.

head.ce:title: Acetophenone substituted phthalocyanines and their graphene quantum dots conjugates as photosensitizers for photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy against Staphylococcus aureus

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Makhanda 6140, South Africa.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports on the synthesis and characterization of novel acetophenone substituted phthalocyanines along with the self-assembled nanoconjugates formed via π-π stacking interaction between the synthesized unmetalated (2), zinc (3) and indium (4) phthalocyanines and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form 2@GQDs, 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs. The complexes and conjugates exhibited high singlet oxygen ranging from 0.20 to 0.79 in DMSO for Pcs and nanoconjugates where in all cases, the indium complexes showed the highest singlet oxygen quantum yields. The photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy activity of both phthalocyanines and nanoconjugates were tested against Staphylococcus aureus. 4@GQDs was found to be highly effective causing a 9.68 log reduction of the bacteria at 10 μM (based on Pc) when compared to 3.77 log reduction of 3@GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Multidrug resistance by microorganisms (such as Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) [1] has limited the use of some of the commercially available drugs for the treatment of infections caused by pathogens. Photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy (PACT), also termed Antimicrobial Photodynamic Therapy (a-PDT), is a potential alternative treatment to address drug resistance by pathogens [2–10].

In PACT a photosensitizer molecule absorbs energy from light of a specific wavelength. The photoexcited photosensitizer in its triplet state interacts with triplet (ground) state molecular oxygen to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as singlet oxygen, that destroy cells or force them into death pathways [2–13]. Phthalocyanine (Pc) dyes are known photosensitizers for PACT [5,7,12].

Pc dyes have recently caught the attention of scientists for applications in PACT due to their physical and chemical stability [14,15], and good production of reactive oxygen species such as singlet oxygen [16–21]. Metallophthalocyanines (MPcs) that have diamagnetic central metals (such as Zn2+, Si4+, In3+, Ga3+, etc.) give high singlet oxygen quantum yields [22,23], and are favored for PACT. Therefore, this study reports the synthesis and use for PACT of Zn (II) and In (III) Pcs as well as the metal free derivative.

Previous reports have proved that non-covalently linking MPcs to carbon nanomaterials such graphene quantum dots (GQDs) may result in the enhancement of their photophysicochemical properties which are very important for PACT. GQDs, which are carbon based nanomaterials with planar π-electron rich structure, are gaining attraction due to their various properties such as stable photoluminescence, low toxicity, chemical stability, high singlet oxygen production and their good ability to form π-π interaction with other π-electron rich molecules such as phthalocyanines [24–31].

In this work, we report on the synthesis of novel acetophenone substituted MPcs: [2,9(10), 16(17), 23(24)-tetrakis-(4-acetophenoxy) phthalocyaninato (TAPPc, 2); 2,9(10), 16(17), 23(24)-tetrakis-(4-acetophenoxy) phthalocyaninato zinc(II) (ZnTAPPc, 3) and 2,9(10), 16(17), 23(24)-tetrakis-(4-acetophenoxy) phthalocyaninato indium (III) chloride (InClTAPPc, 4)], Scheme 1 with high singlet oxygen quantum yields and the formation their nanoassemblies by π-π stacking interaction to GQDs. The linkage of MPcs to GQDs has been done before, but with applications mostly toward photodynamic therapy (PDT) [32], non-linear optics [33], electrocatalysis [34], optical sensing [30], just to cite a few. In this work, we report for the first time, on the use of these type of nanocomposites (MPcs-GQDs) for applications in photodynamic antimicrobial chemotherapy against Stapylococcus aureus, a gram positive bacteria. Also note that GQDs and the acetophenoxy substituents are chosen in this study due to their earlier reported antibacterial properties with reactive carbonyl group [35,36].

4-Hydroxyacetophenone, 4-nitrophthalonitrile, zinc (II) chloride anhydrous, indium (III) chloride and lithium (I) chloride anhydrous salts, diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), 1,8-diazabicyclo[5.4.0]undec-7-ene (DBU), Ludox, deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO-d6), and dimethylaminoethanol (DMAE) were acquired from Sigma Aldrich. Some chemicals such as dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), tetrahydrofuran (THF), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dichloromethane (DCM), ethanol, chloroform were purchased from Merck. All other reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers and were of analytical grade and used without any further purification. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution pH 7.4 was prepared using appropriate amounts of Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4 in ultra-pure millipore water from ELGA, Veolia water PURELAB, flex system (Marlow, UK). Millipore water was employed for all aqueous solutions. Nutrient agar and agar broth were purchased from Merck and prepared as specified by the suppliers. Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC) was obtained from Davies Diagnostics. Graphene quantum dots GQDs were synthesized as previously reported in literature [25].

ce:para.ce:bold: Equipment items used in this work can be found in the Supporting Information

ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of phthalonitrile 1 has been previously reported in literature [37] as follows: 4-hydroxyacetophenone (1 g, 0.0073 mol) and 4-nitrophthalonitrile (1.27 g, 0.0073 mol) were dissolved in 20 mL of DMF under argon atmosphere and stirred for 15 min after which anhydrous K2CO3 (1.521 g, 0.1100 mol) was added to the solution while stirring efficiently for 1.5 h. The reaction mixture was then left to stir under argon atmosphere at 60 °C for 16 h. The completion of the reaction was followed by thin layer chromatography (TLC) using chloroform/ethanol (10:1 v/v) solvent system. The reaction mixture was poured in ice-water (200 mL), the precipitate was filtered off, washed with (3 × 50 mL) water and then precipitated in methanol to give the target compound 1 in 80 % yield as a white solid, IR (KBr, v, cm−1): 3045, 3035 (ArCH), 2917 (Aliph CH and intermolecular H bonds), 2231 (–CN), 1666 (CO), 1577 (CC), 1490-1416 (CC), 1258–1168 (Asym., ArOAr), 1103 (Sym., ArOAr), 835. 1H NMR (600 MHz, CDCl3): δ 8.09 (d, J =8 Hz, 1 H) 7.80 (d, J =12 Hz, 2 H), 7.37 (s, 1 H), 7.33 (d, J =8 Hz, 1 H), 7.17 (d, J =12 Hz, 2 H), 2.65 (s, 3 H) ppm. 13C NMR (600 MHz, CDCl3): δ = 196.6, 159.7, 158.07, 136.4, 134.4, 134.0, 133.8, 131.0, 123.8, 123.4, 119.6, 116.9, 115.8, 115.3, 109.3 and 26.7 ppm.

A mixture of compound 1 (0.5 g, 0.002 mol) and anhydrous lithium chloride (0.081 g, 0.002 mol) was dissolved in 2 mL of dry DMAE and a few drops of DBU were added under argon atmosphere while stirring. The temperature of the reaction was increased to the 140 °C and the stirring continued for 16 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was poured in water-acetic acid (1:1 v/v) mixture and stirred overnight. The resulting mixture was centrifuged and the solid collected was washed several times with water-ethanol (1:1 v/v) mixture then dissolved in DMSO and precipitated out with water-ethanol three times to get a pure free base Pc as green solid.

Under an argon atmosphere, a mixture of anhydrous zinc (II) acetate (0.07 g, 0.00038 mol), compound 1 (0.2 g, 0.0007 mol) and a catalytic amount of DBU in 2 mL of dry DMAE was heated at 160 °C in a sealed glass tube for 16 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was precipitated out by adding ethanol. The formed precipitate was collected by centrifugation, the crude product was dissolved in DMSO and precipitated out with water-ethanol then centrifuged. The pure ZnPc (3) was obtained as a green solid and dried in vacuum.

Under an argon atmosphere, a mixture of compound (2) (0.15 g, 0.00014 mol) and DBU (0.043 g, 0.00028 mol) in 3 mL of dry DMF was left to stir for 30 min then anhydrous InCl3 (0.095 g, 0.00042 mol) was added to the mixture, followed by heating at 150 °C for 2 h. The completion of the reaction was monitored by UV–vis spectroscopy. Once the reaction was completed, the mixture was left to cool to room temperature, and the final product was precipitated out by adding ethanol. The resulting precipitate was washed with methanol several times and centrifuged. The obtained product was puriﬁed using DMSO as explained above for ZnPc (3) method, then finally dried in vacuum. The pure InPc (4) was obtained as a green solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linkage of Pcs complexes to GQDs, (Scheme 2)
ce:section.ce:para: The conjugation of Pc complexes to the GQDs surface was done through the π-π stacking interaction as described for the non-covalent assemblies of other Pcs to GQDs with slight modifications [32] (Scheme 2). For each Pc complex, 30 mg was dissolved in dry DMF, and a solution of GQDs (10 mg/mL) in 2 mL DMF was added. The mixtures were sonicated for 4 h, then left to stir for 4 days at room temperature. The formed conjugates were precipitated out and washed repeatedly with ethanol to remove the unreacted starting material, then dried under vacuum. The conjugates are represented as 2@GQDs, 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs.

Investigation and assessment of photophysicochemical parameters such as fluorescence (ΦF), triplet (ΦT) and singlet oxygen (ΦΔ) quantum yield were performed by comparative methods as described in literature with equations shown in the Supporting information. ZnPc was used as a standard in DMSO with ΦF = 0.2 [38], ΦΔ = 0.67 [16] and ΦT = 0.65 [16].

In this study, firstly the syntheses of phthalocyanine complexes 2, 3 and 4 (Scheme 1) were achieved and then their conjugates were obtained by π-π stacking each one of them onto the GQDs surface (Scheme 2). The precursor compound (1) was synthesized by the reaction of 4-hydroxyacetophenone and 4-nitrophthalonitrile (Scheme 1). This reaction was accomplished through base-catalyzed aromatic substitution reaction under argon atmosphere by using K2CO3 as the base in dry DMF at 60 °C for 16 h [39]. The formation of the metal free and zinc phthalocyanines (2 and 3) was achieved by cyclotetramerization reaction [40] of compound 1 in DMAE and DBU using anhydrous lithium chloride or zinc acetate respectively for metal free and zinc phthalocyanines. After obtaining the H2Pc in this condition, acetic acid was employed to remove lithium metal from inner core. The indium complex (4) was obtained from the metalation reaction of the metal free Pc (2) in dry DMF using DBU as a base and anhydrous indium chloride (Scheme 1). The synthesized phthalocyanines (2, 3 and 4) were soluble in acetone, CHCI3, THF, DMF and DMSO and were characterized by UV–vis, FTIR, 1H-NMR and MALDI-TOF-MS (see Figs. S1–S4, in Supporting information for NMR and mass spectra).

The 1H NMR spectra for TAPPc (2), ZnTAPPc (3), and InTAPPc (4) and exhibited aromatic proton peaks at 8.09-7.15 ppm, 8.51-7.42 ppm and 8.52-7.47 ppm respectively. Methyl protons were observed at 2.58, 2.85 and 2.85 ppm as singlets respectively, while TAPPc (2) exhibited additional peak in the aliphatic region which is assigned to the inner core N-H protons at 5.64 ppm. MALDI-TOF MS was used to acquire the mass spectra of the Pcs. The expected molecular ion peaks were obtained at m/z 1052.44 [M + 1]+ for 2, m/z 1115.58 [M + 1]+ for 3 and 1164.55 [M-Cl+H]+ for 4. These results were in agreement with the suggested structures (Figs. S1–S3, in Supporting information).

The UV–vis spectra of Pc complexes showed the characteristic Q-band and B-band absorption around 680–700 nm and 350–360 nm [18], respectively. Due to D4h symmetry of metalated phthalocyanines, ZnTAPPc (3) and InTAPPc (4) showed narrow monomeric Q bands at nm at 675 and 685 nm, respectively, in DMSO, while complex TAPPc (2) showed a split Q band at 668 and 698 nm due to D2h symmetry [41], Table 1 . Complex 2 showed some aggregation as judged by the enhancement in the 630 nm region [18]. Aggregation in MPcs is judged by a broad and split Q band due to π-π stacking of the macrocycles [18]. Red shifts were observed for 4 as compared to 3 due to the non-planar effect of the indium (III) ion, with a relatively bigger atomic radius than the zinc (II) as the central metal ion in the Pc cavity [42] as shown in Fig. 1 .

After conjugation of Pcs to GQDs (Scheme 2), the enhancement of the B bands for all Pcs in the presence of GQDs (Figs. 2 and S5) implies the presence of GQDs. There are only slight red shifts in the Q bands, Table 1. Red shifts in porphyrin–graphene oxide nanoconjugates have been attributed to molecular flattening [43,44]. Red shifts were also observed in the tetrasulfonated zinc Pc–graphene complex and were attributed to a J type aggregation [45]. The enhancement in the absorption spectra between 600 and 650 nm (Figs. 2 and S5) is attributed to some aggregation for 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs. Aggregation observed in the presence of GQDs could be due to the extensive π-π stacking between adjacent nanoparticles. Comparing the spectra of 2@GQDs, 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs, shows 2@GQDs (Fig. S5) to be more aggregated as was the case for complex 2 alone (Fig. 1). As shown in Fig. 3 , the spectrum for GQDs exhibited a typical broad absorption peak at 343 nm.

Loading of the Pcs derivatives onto the GQDs was accomplished following previous studies using absorption instead of fluorescence [44,46]. This method is based on comparing the Q band absorbance intensities of the nano-assemblies (Pc@GQDs) with that of the respective Pc before the conjugation. Equal masses (mg) for Pc and Pc@GQDs were separately weighed and dissolved in the same volume of solvent. The mass loadings of Pcs onto each GQDs particle were calculated to be: 493, 58, 208 μg (Pc)/mg GQDs for 2@GQDs, 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs, respectively. There is higher loading for 2@GQDs, which results in high aggregation observed as compared to 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs. This could be explained by the fact that the shape of an aromatic molecule strongly affects the π-π interaction, since it limits the distance and orientation between the molecule and a graphene sheet [47].

The FT-IR spectra were employed to determine the surface groups and analyze the chemical components of the GQDs, Pcs alone and the nanoconjugates materials (Figs. 4 and S6 in Supporting information). After the cyclotetramerization reaction, the formation of the phthalocyanines was proved by the disappearance of the sharp -C≡N vibration of the phthalonitrile at 2231 cm−1. The inner core NH hydrogens were observed for compound 2 around 3372 cm−1. Peaks emerging at 2918 cm−1 to 2853 cm−1 for complexes 3 and 4, and 2920 to 2853 cm−1 for 2, are attributed to stretching vibrations of CH bonds. The CH peaks became weaker following conjugation. The peaks at 1667−1586 cm−1 are due to CC and CN vibrations while the CO vibrations are observed at 1714 and 1715 cm−1 for the Pcs alone. The CO peaks shifted to lower frequencies following conjugation of the Pcs to the GQDs. Shifts in FTIR imply molecular interactions. The FT-IR spectra of the conjugates revealed the presence of broad vibration peak at 3330 cm–1 associated with OH stretching (Figs. 4 and S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS)
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of the GQDs alone and the Pcs@GQDs nanoconjugates were determined by using TEM and DLS techniques, Figs. 6 and S7 in Supporting information. Fig. 6a and Fig. S7 show that the GQDs alone are monodispersed and have size ranging from 9 to 11 nm. However, upon conjugation, an increase on the sizes of Pcs@GQDs conjugates is observed as the sizes become 16 nm, 24 nm and 35 nm for 2@GQDs, 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs, respectively. 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs showed less aggregation as compared to 2@GQDs, even though 3@GQDs has no axial ligand. 3@GQDs showed less aggregation than 4@GQDs. This could be due to the fact that 3@GQDs had the lowest loading as shown above. DLS sizes were 10 nm for GQDs alone, and 15.7 nm, 24.4 nm, and 32.7 nm for 2@GQDs, 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs, respectively, Fig. 6B. Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission, Fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) and Fluorescence lifetime (τF)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission, absorption and excitation spectra of Pcs are shown in Figs. 7 and S8 using complexes 3 and 4, respectively, as examples. In all cases, the emission spectra were mirror images of the excitation spectra of studied complexes and the latter were the same as the absorption spectra showing that the molecules that are emitting are the same as those that are absorbing (Figs. 7 and S8, in Supporting information).

Upon excitation of 2 and 2@GQDs at 613 and 609 nm respectively; 3 and 3@GQDs at 613 and 610 nm respectively, and 4 and 4@GQDs at 609 and 611 nm respectively, the fluorescence emission wavelengths were obtained at 706 and 707 nm for 2 and 2@GQDs; at 687 and 688 nm for 3 and 3@GQDs and at 688 and 699 nm for 4 and 4@GQDs. It is important to note that metal free Pcs are known to emit with only one main peak in non-aqueous media which has been assigned as the 0–0 transition [51] hence one emission peak is observed for complex 2, Table 1.

Table 1 shows that TAPPc (2) has the largest ΦF value of 0.20, while ZnTAPPc (3) and InClTAPPc (4) have values of 0.12 and 0.046 respectively. The low ΦF values of ZnTAPPc (3) and InClTAPPc (4) are due the effect of heavy central metals. Heavy metals are known to enhance the intersystem-crossing to the triplet state, thus reducing fluorescence [52]. A monoexponential fluorescence decay curve was obtained for all complexes by employing the time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC), Fig. 8 . The fluorescence lifetimes τF
are shorter 2.11 ns for 3 and 0.89 ns for 4 compared to 4.20 ns for 2 for the same reason given for fluorescence quantum yields. When exciting where Pcs absorb in the conjugates, there is a decrease in both ΦF and τF
values of 2@GQDs and 3@GQDs compared to 2 and 3 alone, but an increase for 4. Increases or decreases in the fluorescence lifetimes may depend on the geometry or distance between the metal and Pc molecule [53]. Aggregation also leads to decrease of emission efficiency and shortening of fluorescence lifetimes [54].

The GQDs alone had ΦF and τF
values of 0.35 and 6.1 ns respectively. Following conjugation to Pcs (when exciting where GQDs absorb), there was a decrease in these values as illustrated in Table 1. The decrease in fluorescence quantum yields of the GQDs in the presence of Pc complexes could be due to the Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and other processes which deactivate the excited states [55].

FRET efficiencies were estimated using fluorescence quantum yields and Equation 5 in Supporting information to be 0.89, 0.88, and 0.91 for 2@GQDs, 3@GQDs, and 4@GQDs, respectively, Table 1. It is however important to clarify that the obtained values are only estimations as there are a numerous other factors that may have resulted in the decrease in the fluorescence parameters [55] of GQDs. The Eff values are not too different suggesting similar spectral overlap.

ΦΔ value is the main factor used to determine whether a photosensitizer is suitable for use in PACT or not. Singlet oxygen, which is toxic to bacteria cells, is produced through the energy transfer from the triplet state of a photosensitizer to the ground state molecular oxygen [16].

In this study, during the determination of singlet oxygen quantum yields, no photobleaching of the Pc complexes or conjugates was observed, Fig. 9 . The ΦΔ values in Table 1 range between 0.20 and 0.79. The higher values of ΦΔ = 0.72 and ΦΔ = 0.75 were observed for ZnTAPPc (3) and InClTAPPc (4), respectively, compared to TAPPc (2) at ΦΔ = 0.22, due to presence of heavy central metals in 3 and 4. It is known the presence of heavy metal ions in the structures induce the intersystem crossing from singlet exited state to the triplet state (from where singlet oxygen is generated) [56]. In addition, acetophenone compounds are known to generate high singlet oxygen quantum yields due to their reactive carbonyl group in their molecular structure [57]. Upon conjugation to GQDs, the ΦΔ values for the nanoconjugates were found to be higher for 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs compared for 3 and 4 alone. However, there was not much change between 2 and 2@GQDs, which could be due to aggregation discussed above and shown in Fig. S5. An increase in ΦΔ in the presence of GQDs could be due to the electron donating ability of GQDs, since electron donating groups are known to increase intersystem crossing in porphyrin-like complexes [58]. The capability of Pcs alone and their GQDs conjugates to generate such high ΦΔ values make them good candidates for PACT applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triplet quantum yield and triplet lifetime
ce:section.ce:para: The triplet quantum yield (ΦT) refers to the fraction of the excited molecules which go to the excited triplet state by intersystem crossing. The triplet decay curve is shown in Fig. 10 . The ΦT values of the synthesized phthalocyanines and their conjugates were determined using comparative methods found in Supporting information using DMSO and the values for ΦT and τT are shown in Table 1. The metallated Pcs and conjugates (3, 4, 3@GQDs and 4@GQDs) exhibited higher triplet quantum yield with values of 0.78, 0.84, 0.85 and 0.90 respectively, compared to metal free compounds (2 and 2@GQDs) at 0.31 and 0.27, respectively. This can be explained by the heavy central atom effect as specified before. It was observed that the ΦT of the nanoconjugates significantly increased compared to the Pcs alone except for compound 2 and its conjugate where there is a decrease following conjugation. The increase in ΦT values of Pcs following conjugation as a result of the reasons given above [58] for the increase in singlet oxygen quantum yield. The decrease of the ΦT value for 2@GQDs compared to 2, is due to aggregation which more severe in the former. Aggregation reduces the excited state lifetimes and the photosensitizing efficiency, probably due to enhanced radiationless excited state dissipation which therefore lowers the quantum yields of the excited states. The triplet lifetimes values were observed in the range of 121−387 μs (Table 1), and they decreased with increase in ΦT values, except for complex 2.

Previous reports have explained that the inactivation of the bacteria takes place through outer membrane [59]. Gram positive bacteria have a cell wall consisting of a cytoplasmic membrane surrounded by a relatively porous peptidoglycan layer and lipoteichoic acid that facilitates the easier penetration of the photosensitisers to the inner membrane [59]. All, neutral, anionic or cationic photosensitisers have the ability to inactivate gram-positive bacteria such as Staphylococcus aureus [59].

Photodynamic antimicrobial therapy has been proven to be better alternative to antibiotics since PACT has been demonstrated to kill microorganisms effectively without them developing resistance to it [60,61]. Therefore, the main research objective remains the development of photosensitisers that have strong photo-antibacterial potency.

In this work, 10 μM (based on Pc) of each complex/conjugate was dissolved in 5 % DMSO in PBS and the later without the photosensitiser was used as a control, and it had no effect on the bacteria. Studies were conducted in the dark to determine if the photosensitisers had dark toxicity, and there was no significant change in log (CFU, colony forming units) in Fig. 11 A, showing that the complexes and conjugates have no dark toxicity. Following PACT studies, Fig. 11B show that 4@GQDs had effectively killed the bacteria when irradiated at 670 nm causing a log reduction of 9.68 and cell survival of 0 % while 2@GQDs displayed the lowest log reduction value of 0.13 with highest 75 % of cell survival, Table 2 . The survival percentage (viable colonies) of the bacteria is determined by the number of remaining alive colonies after treatment of the cells. Fig. 12 represents the bar graph of survival percentage of S.aureus performed with light for the conjugates. Since PACT is based on singlet oxygen production, the obtained results in this study are in agreement with singlet oxygen values obtained and also this shows the importance of conjugation of photosensitizers to nanoparticles, with 4 and 4@GQDs showing better PACT activity corresponding to high singlet oxygen quantum yields. 2@GQDs performed worse than 2 alone due to the extensive aggregation in the former. 3@GQDs at 60 min also performed worse than 3 alone. This could also be a result of aggregation even though there was a slight increase in singlet oxygen quantum yield.

Novel phthalocyanine molecules with peripheral acetophenone substitutuents were successfully synthesized and characterized and their GQDs conjugates were obtained through π-π interaction. The photophysicochemical behaviour of both the Pcs and the conjugates were studied. Zinc and indium complexes showed high singlet oxygen quantum yields. This work also explored the antimicrobial activity of the complexes using PACT while irradiating at 670 nm. It was demonstrated that the indium conjugate complex had better activity against S. Aureus, with the highest log reductions and lowest cell survival.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) Innovation and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University, the Organization for Women in Science for the Developing World (OWSD) and Swedish International Development Cooperation Agency (Sida).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pdpdt.2019.101607.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

J. Chiropr. Med.
